faqs.question: What is the difference between a joint family and a nuclear family?
faqs.answer: A joint family has grandparents, parents, uncles, aunts, and cousins all living together under one roof. Everyone shares meals, celebrates festivals together, and helps each other. A nuclear family has only parents and their children. In India, joint families were more common earlier, but now many families in cities are nuclear families. Both types have their own advantages - joint families have more people to help and play with, while nuclear families have more independence.

faqs.question: What are maternal and paternal grandparents?
faqs.answer: Maternal grandparents are your mother's parents. In Hindi, they are called Nana (grandfather) and Nani (grandmother). Paternal grandparents are your father's parents. In Hindi, they are called Dada (grandfather) and Dadi (grandmother). Different languages in India have different words - in Tamil, both are called Thatha (grandfather) and Paatti (grandmother), in Bengali they are called Dadu and Thamma (paternal) or Dida and Didima (maternal).

faqs.question: How do I make a family tree?
faqs.answer: Making a family tree is fun! Start by writing your name at the bottom of a page. Above you, write your parents' names. Above them, write your grandparents' names. Draw lines connecting children to their parents. Add your brothers, sisters, and cousins in the same row as you. Add uncles and aunts in the same row as your parents. You can also add photos! Ask your parents and grandparents to help you fill in the names.

faqs.question: Why do we have different names for each relative in Hindi?
faqs.answer: Hindi (and many other Indian languages) has specific names for each family relationship because family is very important in Indian culture. In English, 'uncle' can mean father's brother or mother's brother, but in Hindi, father's elder brother is Tauji, father's younger brother is Chacha, and mother's brother is Mama. This makes it very clear exactly how someone is related to you. It shows how detailed and rich Indian family systems are!

faqs.question: How is life in a village different from life in a city?
faqs.answer: In a village, houses often have courtyards and gardens. People may keep animals like cows, goats, and hens. Everyone knows their neighbours. Food is often fresh from the farm. The air is cleaner and there are open fields to play in. In a city, houses are closer together, people travel by car or metro, food comes from shops, and there are malls, schools, and hospitals nearby. Both places have good things and challenges!

faqs.question: What special things do grandparents usually cook?
faqs.answer: Grandparents often cook traditional dishes that have been passed down for generations! Every family has special recipes - it could be Dadi's famous dal, Nani's special laddoos, Paatti's sambar, or Ajji's akki roti. These recipes are often made with love and patience, and they taste different from restaurant or regular food. Ask your grandparents to teach you their favourite recipe - you will be preserving a family tradition!

faqs.question: What games did our grandparents play when they were young?
faqs.answer: Our grandparents played many games that did not need phones or computers! They played hopscotch (stapu), marbles (kanche), spinning tops (lattoo), seven stones (pitthoo), kabaddi, gilli-danda, and many more traditional games. They also climbed trees, swam in rivers, and played with animals. Ask your grandparents about the games they played - you might learn a game you have never tried before!

faqs.question: Why is it important to listen to stories from our elders?
faqs.answer: Stories from elders are valuable because they carry history, wisdom, and family memories. Through stories, grandparents teach us about our culture, our family's past, and important life lessons. These stories are often not written in any book - if we do not listen and remember them, they will be lost forever. Elders also share how the world has changed, helping us appreciate what we have today.

faqs.question: How has life changed from our grandparents' time to now?
faqs.answer: Life has changed a lot! When grandparents were young, there were no mobile phones, internet, or TV in most homes. They wrote letters instead of sending messages. They walked or cycled to school instead of going by car. Food was cooked on wood or coal stoves instead of gas or electric stoves. They got water from wells instead of taps. But some things remain the same - love for family, festivals, and good food!

faqs.question: What if my grandparents live far away and I cannot visit them often?
faqs.answer: Even if your grandparents live far away, you can stay connected! You can talk to them on the phone or video call. You can write them letters or draw pictures to send by post. You can share photos and videos. When you do visit, make the most of it - sit with them, listen to their stories, cook together, and take lots of photos. Some children also record their grandparents' stories on their parents' phone, so they can listen again later!
